chunk_summary: Guidance on nutrition-first management of growth faltering in infants over six months, focusing on meal frequency, energy density, responsive feeding, and screening for medical contributors using WHO growth standards.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of meal frequency, energy density, and responsive feeding in addressing growth faltering after six months.
Learn how to use WHO growth standards to assess weight-for-age and weight-for-length trends.
Identify common medical contributors to growth faltering and appropriate screening methods.
Implement dietary optimization strategies, including age-appropriate feeding targets and nutrient-rich food additions.
key_facts: Recommended meal frequency: 6–8 months: 2–3 meals; 9–11 months: 3 meals + snacks; 12–24 months: 3 meals + 2 snacks.
Limit cow’s milk intake to <500 mL/day and eliminate juice and sugary drinks.
Enhance energy density by adding ghee/oil, peanut powder, or dairy to staples.
Screen for anemia, recurrent infections, chronic diarrhea, cow’s milk protein allergy, tuberculosis exposure, and congenital heart disease if dietary optimization fails.
Urgently evaluate for severe wasting, nutritional edema, or feeding refusal with lethargy as these may indicate severe acute malnutrition (SAM) or systemic illness.
reference_quote: Plot weight-for-age and weight-for-length using WHO growth standards and look for growth velocity; a single low percentile is less concerning than downward crossing.
document_key_topics: Age-appropriate feeding targets and meal frequency
Screening for medical contributors to growth faltering
Responsive feeding and dietary optimization strategies
